textPlain: Anyone can recommend a good and straightforward to understand -- general -- tutorial or book in Monte Carlo methods, for beginners?
If you're interested in HMC, we showed how to apply it to the Ising model in https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.03278 with code available in https://github.com/HISKP-LQCD/ising_hmc
R ecosystem provides amazing reproducible research ecosystem, even for statistical physics.
Does the use of "Statistical Physics" as opposed to "Statistical Mechanics" indicates a European author or a broader scope?

The "Monte Carlo" term is deceivingly fancy, most algorithms with that label simply involve randomly picking from a set of possibilities (search space), as opposed to trying all cases or exploring in some other systematic way.When you try do that for real problems, it can sometimes be difficult to sample from complex probability distributions/models efficiently in a way that is representative. But it still boils down to randomly testing options.Look at the source code, even in C it's really short and simple:
https://github.com/msuzen/isingLenzMC/blob/master/src/isingL...
I wonder how close R was to also take over the scientific computing/machine learning space, instead of Python's numpy/scipy ecosystem.
